author: Robert Carr
description: A reverse-seared thick-cut Wagyu-style ribeye using a measured salt dry brine, low oven cook, final dry pat, and hard stainless steel pan sear with an optional brief garlic-thyme butter baste.
ingredients: 1 thick-cut ribeye steak, about 1 pound and at least 1 1/2 inches thick
3.4 grams kosher salt, about 0.75% of the steak weight
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground, added after searing or lightly before oven step
1 to 2 teaspoons avocado oil, or another neutral high-heat oil
1 pinch flaky salt, optional, for finishing
instructions: Thaw steak fully in the refrigerator if frozen.
Weigh the steak in grams if possible.
Calculate kosher salt as 0.75% of the steak weight. Use the formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For a 1 pound steak, use about 3.4 grams kosher salt total for the top, bottom, edges, and fat cap.
Remove steak from packaging and pat very dry on all sides with paper towels.
Season steak evenly on all sides with the measured kosher salt only.
Place a wire rack over a baking sheet or plate, then place the steak on the wire rack uncovered.
Refrigerate uncovered for 4 to 8 hours, or up to 12 hours if using the conservative 0.75% salt amount. This dry-brines the steak and dries the surface for a better crust.
Remove steak from the refrigerator 30 to 45 minutes before cooking.
Pat steak dry again if any surface moisture appears.
Preheat oven to 225°F.
Place steak on a wire rack over a baking sheet and cook in the oven until the center reaches 115°F to 118°F for classic medium-rare, or 118°F to 120°F for medium-rare leaning medium.

Begin checking the internal temperature around 20 minutes using an instant-read thermometer inserted into the center from the side. Continue checking every 5 minutes, then every 2 to 3 minutes once close to the target temperature.
Remove steak from oven and rest for 5 to 10 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board while preheating the stainless steel pan.
Preheat a stainless steel pan on induction level 6.5 to 7 until a few drops of water bead and glide across the pan.
Just before searing, pat the steak very dry on all sides again.
Wipe out any water from the pan, add avocado oil, and heat until shimmering.
If the steak has a pronounced fat cap, hold it with tongs and render the fat cap for 30 to 60 seconds first.
Place steak in the pan and sear the broad sides for 45 to 75 seconds per side, pressing gently to ensure good pan contact.
Sear any remaining edges using tongs until browned.
Reduce heat slightly, add butter, smashed garlic, and thyme if using, then baste briefly for 20 to 30 seconds so the butter flavors the crust without overwhelming the beef.
Remove steak from pan and rest for 5 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board.
Slice against the grain.
Finish with flaky salt and black pepper if desired.
notes: This method works best with a steak at least 1 1/2 inches thick, and even better around 1 3/4 to 2 inches thick.
Best target for this steak is medium-rare leaning medium, around 132°F to 137°F final internal temperature.
For a fattier Wagyu-style ribeye, avoid rare because the fat may stay waxy instead of softening and rendering.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare, pull from the oven at 115°F to 118°F and finish around 130°F to 135°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare leaning medium, pull from the oven at 118°F to 120°F and finish around 132°F to 137°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium, pull from the oven at 122°F to 125°F and finish around 138°F to 145°F after searing and resting.
Salt before the refrigerator dry brine. Add black pepper after searing or lightly before the oven step because pepper can burn during a hard sear.
Use 0.75% salt by steak weight for a conservative dry brine. Formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For this 1 pound steak, 0.75% salt is about 3.4 grams total.
A lighter 0.5% salt level would be about 2.27 grams for this steak. A normal 1.0% level would be about 4.54 grams.
If using volume instead of weight, 3.4 grams is roughly 1 heaping teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or about 2/3 to 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt.
For best texture, dry brine this steak for 4 to 8 hours. Up to 12 hours is reasonable with the conservative 0.75% salt amount.
Avoid pushing the dry brine much past 12 hours for this steak unless using a very light salt level, because longer or heavier dry brines can make the outer texture seem slightly cured.
If short on time, even 1 hour uncovered in the fridge helps. If there is no time, salt right before cooking and pat the steak extremely dry.
The salt dry brine seasons the steak more deeply. Salt first draws out a little moisture, dissolves, then gets pulled back into the meat over time.
The wire rack matters because it lets air circulate around the steak. A plate traps moisture underneath and keeps the bottom wet.
The uncovered refrigerator step dries the surface. A drier surface browns faster and forms a better crust.
The final dry pat before searing is important because moisture from the oven step can soften the surface and slow browning.
Do not braise this steak. Use the oven only for the low-temperature reverse sear step, then finish in the stainless steel pan.
For the cleanest beef flavor, skip the butter baste or keep it very brief. For a steakhouse finish, use the butter, garlic, and thyme for only the last 20 to 30 seconds.
A laser thermometer is less reliable on shiny stainless steel; the water bead test and steak internal temperature are more useful.
If the pan smokes violently before the steak goes in, lower the induction level slightly.
Leftovers reheat best gently. Warm leftover steak in a 250°F oven on a wire rack over a baking sheet until the center reaches about 100°F to 110°F.
Do not reheat leftover steak back to 130°F or higher unless you are okay with it becoming more cooked than the original steak.
If desired, briefly touch up the crust after reheating by searing the cut sides in a hot pan for 10 to 20 seconds per side.
Leftover steak is also good cold or room temperature, sliced thin with flaky salt, eggs, rice, salad, or a sandwich.
personalNotes: This was really good. The steak was rich and buttery, enough that I was full halfway through. The butter baste is optional; the Wagyu-style ribeye has plenty of fat on its own. I dry-brined it for about 5 hours after thawing from frozen in the fridge. Used Central Market olive-fed Texas Wagyu.
title: Reverse-Seared Wagyu Ribeye
totalTime: 45 minutes plus 4 to 8 hours dry-brine time
